text: The video shows the port placement for the colectomy procedure. The surgeon begins by mobilizing the mesenteric root from medial to lateral at the ligament of Treitz using the uncinate first approach, standing on the patient's left side. The pancreatic head and major vessels are exposed early in the operation. The ascending mesocolon is incised through the peritoneal window, preserving the iliocolic vessels, and the incision continues across the mesenteric root at the base of the transverse mesocolon. The superior mesenteric vein is dissected, and lymphadenectomy is performed up to the base of the mesocolon, revealing the veins of the Heimler trunk, without dividing any large vessels at this stage.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac along the great curvature of the stomach, dividing erythrogastric adhesions to the mesocolon and pancreas. The peritoneum is incised along the first part of the duodenum, allowing stepwise identification and clipping of the gastromental artery. The right gastromental artery is divided, facilitating further mobilization of the mesogastrium from the pancreatic head. The inferior border of the pancreas is dissected, revealing the middle colic vein and superior right colic vein near the Hehler trunk. Bleeding from the right gastromental vein is managed by increasing CO₂ inflation pressure and using bipolar coagulation.

The surgeon continues the operation inferior to the mesocolon, defining the plane in the lesser sac and the lower border of the pancreas. The mesocolic vessels are divided, starting with the encirclement and clipping of the middle colic artery at the level of the AMS. Subsequently, the pancreatic or duodenal vein and the Hamlet trunk at its confluence with the superior mesenteric vein are divided.

The surgeon observes that the middle colic vein joins the splenic vein instead of the superior mesenteric vein. The final step involves dividing the lateral adhesions on the right side of the patient. The view reveals complete lymphatic clearance at the inferior border of the pancreas and around the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon, now positioned on the right side of the patient, performs a medial to lateral mobilization of the splenic flexure, starting posterior to the mesenteric vein. The mesenteric vein is divided, and the transverse mesocolon is dissected away from the tail of the pancreas. The gastromental vascular arcade is completely removed from the stomach up to the spleen. The left colon is mobilized laterally, following Gerota's fascia, reaching the medial dissection plane inferior to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon carefully dissects and selectively divides the left gastromental vessels at the splenic hilum. The small artery is isolated and divided first, followed by the larger vein, which is securely closed and divided using an energy device. Additional adhesions to the splenic hilum are divided, freeing the entire transverse colon. A clip is visible on the left gastromental vein where it joins the splenic vein.

The surgeon dissects and divides the left colic vessels near their origin at the inferior mesenteric artery, managing a small bleeding by increasing CO₂ inflation pressure and clipping. The left colon is mobilized down to the rectosigmoid junction. The final view shows complete clearance of mesocolic and gastro-mental tissue, with the pancreas intact and the stomach well-perfused. The specimen meets criteria for complete mesocolic excision with preserved integrity and long vascular stalks.
